summary:
The Gracchi Brothers Reforms (133–121 BC) were the legislative programs of the Roman tribunes Tiberius and Gaius Gracchus, who attempted to address the severe social dislocations produced by Rome's imperial expansion, initiating a period of political violence that some historians see as the beginning of the end of the Roman Republic. Their careers established the pattern of populares — reform politicians who appealed to the assembly against the Senate — that would culminate in Julius Caesar and Augustus.

Tiberius Gracchus (tribune 133 BC) proposed land reforms to redistribute ager publicus (public land) that wealthy senators had illegally occupied above the legal limit, providing small plots to landless citizens. The reform was constitutionally correct but politically explosive, as it threatened the property of the senatorial class. When a fellow tribune tried to veto the bill, Tiberius had him removed — an unprecedented action. Tiberius was murdered by a mob of senators in the Forum, the first such political killing in Roman history since the monarchy. His body was thrown in the Tiber.

Gaius Gracchus (tribune 123–122 BC) pursued a broader reform agenda including grain subsidies for the urban poor, extending citizenship, strengthening the equestrian (business) class against the Senate, and founding new colonies. He was also killed in political violence in 121 BC — this time in a more organized massacre that killed thousands of his supporters. The Senate later passed the first Senatus consultum ultimum ('ultimate decree'), a emergency provision directing the consuls to 'take measures to preserve the state' that became a tool for using violence against reform politicians.

The Gracchi era established that Roman politics could be resolved by blood rather than procedure — a lesson that the subsequent generation of generals, from Marius to Sulla to Caesar, would take to increasingly extreme lengths. The social problems the Gracchi identified — landless citizens, slave-worked latifundia, Italian allies without citizenship — were not resolved and continued to generate instability throughout the Republic's final century.
